skill.name: verification-protocol
skill.description: Independent verification workflow for proving task completion with evidence instead of agent self-attestation.
skill.summary: Require independent evidence before marking AI agent tasks complete.
skill.author: CleanExpo

security_audit.summary: Static command, filesystem, weak crypto, and reconnaissance findings were reviewed in context. The weak crypto and reconnaissance alerts are false positives from Markdown prose, but the skill intentionally instructs agents to run build, lint, test, filesystem, and endpoint checks, so publication should include a security warning.

medium_findings.title: External Command Execution Guidance
medium_findings.description: The skill directs verifiers to run TypeScript compilation, ESLint, and test commands against task outputs. This is legitimate verification behavior, but it can execute project-controlled tooling and scripts if used without sandboxing.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicitly documented and aligned with the skill purpose. Risk remains because running npm, npx, or test commands can execute repository scripts.

medium_findings.title: Filesystem Inspection of Claimed Paths
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs agents to inspect files and collect file metadata from claimed output paths. This is expected for verification, but path scope must be controlled to avoid reading unrelated files.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The filesystem operations are documentation examples, not executable code. They are still semantically central to the skill and require workspace boundaries.

medium_findings.title: HTTP Endpoint Verification Guidance
medium_findings.description: The skill recommends making HTTP requests to endpoints and checking response status and shape. This can be safe for local health checks, but unrestricted URLs could become network probing.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The text references API and health endpoint verification rather than exfiltration. The risk depends on whether implementations restrict requests to approved local or test endpoints.

low_findings.title: Static Weak Crypto Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The static analyzer reported weak cryptographic algorithm indicators at Markdown prose and example lines. I found no hashing, encryption, or cryptographic API usage in the skill file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line review shows ordinary documentation text and example control flow. No evidence found for MD5, SHA1, DES, or other weak cryptographic operations.

low_findings.title: Static Reconnaissance Alerts Are Documentation Context
low_findings.description: The reconnaissance alerts map to verification failure text and example agent identifiers, not commands that enumerate hosts, users, or network services.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are descriptive Markdown or TypeScript examples for task verification. No evidence found of system or network reconnaissance commands.

dangerous_patterns.title: Project Command Execution
dangerous_patterns.description: Examples instruct verifiers to run npm and npx commands. Use an isolated environment and avoid shell interpolation for claimed file paths.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is directly present in examples and method descriptions. It is dangerous only when implemented without sandboxing and argument validation.

dangerous_patterns.title: Unscoped Endpoint Requests
dangerous_patterns.description: Endpoint verification can generate HTTP traffic. Implementations should restrict checks to local, test, or explicitly approved endpoints.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The skill describes endpoint checks but does not define allowlists. The finding is medium confidence because the examples appear intended for internal health endpoints.

content.value_statement: AI agents can mark tasks complete without real proof. This skill defines independent checks that require files, tests, lint results, and endpoint evidence.
actual_capabilities: Defines an independent verification process for claimed task completion.
Lists evidence types for files, placeholders, compilation, linting, tests, and endpoints.
Provides failure handling with retries and human escalation after repeated failures.
Shows examples of valid and invalid verification results.
Documents how orchestrators should store verification evidence before completion.
limitations: It is a process guide, not a complete verifier implementation.
Command execution must be sandboxed by the host environment.
Endpoint checks require caller-defined allowlists and timeouts.
It does not replace human review for ambiguous failures.

use_cases.title: Review Agent Deliverables
use_cases.description: Require independent proof before accepting files, tests, or implementation work from an AI coding agent.

use_cases.title: Gate Workflow Completion
use_cases.description: Add evidence-based completion checks to orchestrated tasks before moving work to the next stage.

use_cases.title: Audit Quality Signals
use_cases.description: Collect consistent evidence for compilation, linting, tests, placeholders, and endpoint responses.

prompt_templates.title: Verify One Task
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner check for a single completed task.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the verification protocol to review this completed task. Check each criterion with evidence and report pass or fail.

prompt_templates.title: Build Evidence Checklist
prompt_templates.prompt: Create completion criteria for this task using file, placeholder, compile, lint, test, and endpoint evidence where relevant.

prompt_templates.title: Review Failed Verification
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze this verification failure report. Identify the root causes and list the exact fixes required before resubmission.

prompt_templates.title: Design Orchestrator Gate
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced integration into an agent workflow.
prompt_templates.prompt: Design an independent verification gate for this agent workflow. Include evidence storage, retry limits, escalation, and sandbox rules.

output_examples.input: A coding agent claims a new TypeScript file and test are complete.
output_examples.output: The verifier reports file size, placeholder scan results, TypeScript status, lint status, and test pass counts before approving completion.

output_examples.input: A task fails because the expected test file is missing.
output_examples.output: The verifier returns a failed status, names the missing evidence, increments the retry count, and sends the task back for repair.

output_examples.input: An API task requires a health endpoint check.
output_examples.output: The verifier records status code, response time, and response shape from an approved endpoint before marking the criterion as passed.

best_practices: Run verification in a separate agent or process from the original worker.
Capture concrete evidence for every criterion before approving completion.
Sandbox commands and restrict filesystem and network access during checks.
anti_patterns: Do not accept an agent statement as proof of completion.
Do not skip slow tests or endpoint checks without recording a failure.
Do not run project commands against untrusted paths without validation.

faq.question: What does this skill verify?
faq.answer: It verifies task completion claims using independent evidence such as files, tests, lint results, compiler output, and endpoint responses.

faq.question: Does it include executable verifier code?
faq.answer: No. It provides a protocol, examples, and implementation guidance for adding verifier logic to an agent workflow.

faq.question: Why is independent verification required?
faq.answer: The original agent may overstate completion. A separate verifier reduces false success reports by checking real evidence.

faq.question: Can this run shell commands?
faq.answer: The document recommends command-based checks. Implementations should run them only in controlled sandboxes with validated arguments.

faq.question: How are failed checks handled?
faq.answer: The task returns to the worker with failure details. After repeated failures, the protocol escalates to human review.

faq.question: Does it work with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code?
faq.answer: Yes. The protocol is written for agent workflows and can guide Claude, Codex, and Claude Code verification steps.
